The problem says that an index i is beautiful if two conditions are met. First, the substring starting at i matches string a. Second, there exists some index j where the substring matches string b and the absolute difference between j and i is at most k. And then return the sorted list of beautiful indices.

So the first thing I need to do is find all occurrences of a in the string s as possible candidates for i. Similarly, find all occurrences of b as possible j's. Then, for each occurrence of a, check if there's at least one occurrence of b within k distance. But how to do this efficiently?

Wait, but the problem requires that for each i (which is an occurrence of a), there exists at least one occurrence of b such that |j - i| <=k. So the challenge is, given a list of i's (from a) and a list of j's (from b), for each i, check if there's a j in the j-list that is within k distance.

The straightforward approach would be for each occurrence of a, iterate through all occurrences of b and check if any is within the range [i -k, i +k]. But if the number of occurrences is large, this would be O(M*N), which might be too slow for large inputs. Since s can be up to 5e5 in length, and the occurrences could be numerous, this approach might not work.

So how can we optimize this? Well, if we have the list of j's (the occurrences of b) sorted, then for each occurrence of a (let's call it x), we can check if there's any j in the j-list that is in [x -k, x +k]. Since the j-list is sorted, we can perform a binary search to find if there's any such j.

For example, for a given x (an occurrence of a), the valid j's would be those where j >= x -k and j <= x +k. So we can find the first index in the j-list that is >= (x -k) and check if that j is <= (x +k). Also, need to check if there's any such element. If there's at least one, then x is a beautiful index.

So the plan is:

Once we have A and B, then proceed as before.

So first, implement KMP for finding all occurrences of a pattern in a text.

Let me recall how KMP works. The KMP algorithm preprocesses the pattern to create a "failure function" (also called the longest prefix suffix table) which allows the search to proceed in linear time. The failure function for each position indicates the longest proper prefix which is also a suffix. This helps in avoiding backtracking in the text during the search.

The steps for KMP to find all occurrences of p in text s:

1. Preprocess the pattern p to compute the failure function (lps array).
2. Use two pointers, i for the text (s) and j for the pattern (p). Initialize i=0, j=0.
3. While i < s.length():
   a. If s[i] == p[j], increment both i and j.
   b. If j == p.length(), then we found a match at i - j. Add (i-j) to the list of occurrences. Then reset j to lps[j-1] to continue searching for overlapping matches.
   c. Else if i < s.length() and s[i] != p[j], then if j != 0, set j = lps[j-1], else increment i.

This way, the entire search is O(n + m) time.

So, implementing this helper function is crucial.

Now, the helper function for KMP.

Let me code the helper function.

Function to generate the lps array for KMP:

vector<int> computeLPS(const string& p) {
    int m = p.length();
    vector<int> lps(m, 0);
    int len = 0; // length of the previous longest prefix suffix
    for (int i = 1; i < m;) {
        if (p[i] == p[len]) {
            len++;
            lps[i] = len;
            i++;
        } else {
            if (len != 0) {
                len = lps[len - 1];
            } else {
                lps[i] = 0;
                i++;
            }
        }
    }
    return lps;
}

Then, the KMP search function:

vector<int> kmpSearch(const string& text, const string& pattern) {
    vector<int> result;
    if (pattern.empty() || text.length() < pattern.length()) return result;
    vector<int> lps = computeLPS(pattern);
    int i = 0; // index for text
    int j = 0; // index for pattern
    while (i < text.length()) {
        if (pattern[j] == text[i]) {
            i++;
            j++;
        }
        if (j == pattern.length()) {
            // found a match at i - j
            result.push_back(i - j);
            j = lps[j - 1];
        } else if (i < text.length() && pattern[j] != text[i]) {
            if (j != 0) {
                j = lps[j - 1];
            } else {
                i++;
            }
        }
    }
    return result;
}

Wait, but in the KMP code, after finding a match (j == m), we add (i-j) to the result. Then set j = lps[j-1], so that we can check for overlapping matches. For example, in the text "aaaaa" and pattern "aa", the matches would be at 0, 1, 2, 3.

Yes, this code should collect all occurrences.

Now, integrating this into the solution.

So in the code:

class Solution {
public:
    vector<int> beautifulIndices(string s, string a, string b, int k) {
        // Find all occurrences of a and b in s
        vector<int> A = kmpSearch(s, a);
        vector<int> B = kmpSearch(s, b);
        
        // For each occurrence in A, check if there's a j in B within k distance
        vector<int> res;
        for (int x : A) {
            // Check if there exists j in B where |x - j| <=k
            long long low = (long long)x - k;
            long long high = (long long)x + k;
            // Binary search in B for any element in [low, high]
            auto it = lower_bound(B.begin(), B.end(), low);
            if (it != B.end() && *it <= high) {
                res.push_back(x);
            }
        }
        return res;
    }
    
private:
    vector<int> computeLPS(const string& p) {
        int m = p.length();
        vector<int> lps(m, 0);
        int len = 0;
        int i = 1;
        while (i < m) {
            if (p[i] == p[len]) {
                len++;
                lps[i] = len;
                i++;
            } else {
                if (len != 0) {
                    len = lps[len - 1];
                } else {
                    lps[i] = 0;
                    i++;
                }
            }
        }
        return lps;
    }
    
    vector<int> kmpSearch(const string& text, const string& pattern) {
        vector<int> result;
        if (pattern.empty() || text.length() < pattern.length()) return result;
        vector<int> lps = computeLPS(pattern);
        int i = 0; // text index
        int j = 0; // pattern index
        while (i < text.length()) {
            if (pattern[j] == text[i]) {
                i++;
                j++;
            }
            if (j == pattern.length()) {
                result.push_back(i - j);
                j = lps[j - 1];
            } else if (i < text.length() && pattern[j] != text[i]) {
                if (j != 0) {
                    j = lps[j - 1];
                } else {
                    i++;
                }
            }
        }
        return result;
    }
};

vector<int> kmpSearch(const string& text, const string& pattern) {
    vector<int> result;
    if (pattern.empty() || text.length() < pattern.length()) return result;

Now, with this correct KMP code, the A and B lists are generated correctly.

Now, considering the time and space complexity.

Time complexity:

- KMP for a is O(n + m), where n is length of s, m is length of a.
- Similarly for KMP for b: O(n + l), where l is length of b.
- Then, for each occurrence in A (let's say there are O(p) occurrences), perform a binary search on B (which has O(q) elements, occurrences of b). Each binary search is O(log q). So overall O(p log q) for this part.

Total time complexity is O(n + m + l + p log q).

Space complexity is O(q + p) for storing B and A.

Now, considering the constraints:

s.length can be up to 5e5. So for n=5e5, and a and b also up to 5e5, the KMP steps are acceptable.

The binary search part depends on p and q. For example, if p is O(n) and q is O(n), then p log q is O(n log n), which is acceptable.

So overall, the algorithm is efficient.

So the complete code would be:

class Solution {
public:
    vector<int> beautifulIndices(string s, string a, string b, int k) {
        vector<int> A = kmpSearch(s, a);
        vector<int> B = kmpSearch(s, b);
        vector<int> res;
        for (int x : A) {
            long long low = (long long)x - k;
            long long high = (long long)x + k;
            auto it = lower_bound(B.begin(), B.end(), low);
            if (it != B.end() && *it <= high) {
                res.push_back(x);
            }
        }
        return res;
    }
private:
    vector<int> computeLPS(const string& p) {
        int m = p.length();
        vector<int> lps(m, 0);
        int len = 0;
        int i = 1;
        while (i < m) {
            if (p[i] == p[len]) {
                len++;
                lps[i] = len;
                i++;
            } else {
                if (len != 0) {
                    len = lps[len - 1];
                } else {
                    lps[i] = 0;
                    i++;
                }
            }
        }
        return lps;
    }
    
    vector<int> kmpSearch(const string& text, const string& pattern) {
        vector<int> result;
        if (pattern.empty() || text.length() < pattern.length()) return result;
        vector<int> lps = computeLPS(pattern);
        int j = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < (int)text.length(); i++) {
            while (j > 0 && text[i] != pattern[j]) {
                j = lps[j - 1];
            }
            if (text[i] == pattern[j]) {
                j++;
            }
            if (j == (int)pattern.length()) {
                result.push_back(i - j + 1);
                j = lps[j - 1];
            }
        }
        return result;
    }
};

So first, implement KMP for finding all occurrences of a pattern in a text.

Let me recall how KMP works. The KMP algorithm preprocesses the pattern to create a "failure function" (also called the longest prefix suffix table) which allows the search to proceed in linear time. The failure function for each position indicates the longest proper prefix which is also a suffix. This helps in avoiding backtracking in the text during the search.

The steps for KMP to find all occurrences of p in text s:

1. Preprocess the pattern p to compute the failure function (lps array).
2. Use two pointers, i for the text (s) and j for the pattern (p). Initialize i=0, j=0.
3. While i < s.length():
   a. If s[i] == p[j], increment both i and j.
   b. If j == p.length(), then we found a match at i - j. Add (i-j) to the list of occurrences. Then reset j to lps[j-1] to continue searching for overlapping matches.
   c. Else if i < s.length() and s[i] != p[j], then if j != 0, set j = lps[j-1], else increment i.

This way, the entire search is O(n + m) time.

Function to generate the lps array for KMP:

vector<int> computeLPS(const string& p) {
    int m = p.length();
    vector<int> lps(m, 0);
    int len = 0; // length of the previous longest prefix suffix
    for (int i = 1; i < m;) {
        if (p[i] == p[len]) {
            len++;
            lps[i] = len;
            i++;
        } else {
            if (len != 0) {
                len = lps[len - 1];
            } else {
                lps[i] = 0;
                i++;
            }
        }
    }
    return lps;
}

Then, the KMP search function:

vector<int> kmpSearch(const string& text, const string& pattern) {
    vector<int> result;
    if (pattern.empty() || text.length() < pattern.length()) return result;
    vector<int> lps = computeLPS(pattern);
    int i = 0; // index for text
    int j = 0; // index for pattern
    while (i < text.length()) {
        if (pattern[j] == text[i]) {
            i++;
            j++;
        }
        if (j == pattern.length()) {
            // found a match at i - j
            result.push_back(i - j);
            j = lps[j - 1];
        } else if (i < text.length() && pattern[j] != text[i]) {
            if (j != 0) {
                j = lps[j - 1];
            } else {
                i++;
            }
        }
    }
    return result;
}

So in the code:

class Solution {
public:
    vector<int> beautifulIndices(string s, string a, string b, int k) {
        // Find all occurrences of a and b in s
        vector<int> A = kmpSearch(s, a);
        vector<int> B = kmpSearch(s, b);
        
        // For each occurrence in A, check if there's a j in B within k distance
        vector<int> res;
        for (int x : A) {
            // Check if there exists j in B where |x - j| <=k
            long long low = (long long)x - k;
            long long high = (long long)x + k;
            // Binary search in B for any element in [low, high]
            auto it = lower_bound(B.begin(), B.end(), low);
            if (it != B.end() && *it <= high) {
                res.push_back(x);
            }
        }
        return res;
    }
    
private:
    vector<int> computeLPS(const string& p) {
        int m = p.length();
        vector<int> lps(m, 0);
        int len = 0;
        int i = 1;
        while (i < m) {
            if (p[i] == p[len]) {
                len++;
                lps[i] = len;
                i++;
            } else {
                if (len != 0) {
                    len = lps[len - 1];
                } else {
                    lps[i] = 0;
                    i++;
                }
            }
        }
        return lps;
    }
    
    vector<int> kmpSearch(const string& text, const string& pattern) {
        vector<int> result;
        if (pattern.empty() || text.length() < pattern.length()) return result;
        vector<int> lps = computeLPS(pattern);
        int i = 0; // text index
        int j = 0; // pattern index
        while (i < text.length()) {
            if (pattern[j] == text[i]) {
                i++;
                j++;
            }
            if (j == pattern.length()) {
                result.push_back(i - j);
                j = lps[j - 1];
            } else if (i < text.length() && pattern[j] != text[i]) {
                if (j != 0) {
                    j = lps[j - 1];
                } else {
                    i++;
                }
            }
        }
        return result;
    }
};

vector<int> kmpSearch(const string& text, const string& pattern) {
    vector<int> result;
    if (pattern.empty() || text.length() < pattern.length()) return result;
    vector<int> lps = computeLPS(pattern);
    int j = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < (int)text.length(); i++) {
        while (j > 0 && text[i] != pattern[j]) {
            j = lps[j-1];
        }
        if (text[i] == pattern[j]) {
            j++;
        } else {
            // no need to do anything, j remains 0
        }
        if (j == (int)pattern.length()) {
            result.push_back(i - j + 1);
            j = lps[j-1];
        }
    }
    return result;
}

This should correctly find all occurrences of the pattern in the text.

Now, with this correct KMP code, the A and B lists are generated correctly.
